title: What Happens If I Am Waitlisted at My Dream University?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You opened the admissions decision. You did not see congratulations. You did not see we regret to inform you. You saw something in between: you have been placed on the waitlist. It feels like purgatory. You are good enough to not be rejected, but not quite good enough to be accepted. Now what? Being waitlisted is not the end of the road, but it requires a specific strategy. Here is what a waitlist actually means, what your odds are, and how to write a Letter of Continued Interest that might tip the scales in your favor.

What the Waitlist Actually Means

Universities use waitlists to manage enrollment uncertainty. They accept more students than they can enroll because they know not everyone will say yes. If fewer accepted students enroll than expected, the university goes to the waitlist to fill the remaining spots. You are on the waitlist because the admissions committee believes you are qualified to attend. They simply did not have room for you in the initial round.

Waitlist acceptance rates vary dramatically by school. At highly selective institutions, the waitlist acceptance rate can be in the single digits or even zero in some years. At larger universities, especially public institutions, the waitlist moves more significantly. Some schools admit hundreds of students from the waitlist. Others admit none. The only way to know your specific situation is to research the historical waitlist data for that institution. Many schools publish this information in their Common Data Set, which is available online.

Step One: Accept Your Spot on the Waitlist

If you want to remain in consideration, you must formally accept your position on the waitlist. There is usually a deadline to do this. Miss it, and you are removed from consideration. Accept the spot even if you are not sure you would attend if admitted. You can always decline later. Right now, you want to keep the option open.

Step Two: Commit to Another School

This is the hardest emotional step, but it is essential. Put down a deposit at a school where you have been accepted. The waitlist is not a guarantee. It is not even a probability at most schools. You need a real plan for the fall. Find a school on your acceptance list that you can genuinely see yourself attending. Pay the enrollment deposit. Sign up for housing. Get excited about it.

If you get off the waitlist later, you will lose your deposit at the other school. That is the cost of keeping your options open. A few hundred dollars is a small price compared to the cost of attending the wrong school or having no school to attend at all.

Step Three: Write a Letter of Continued Interest

This is the most important action you can take. A Letter of Continued Interest, or LOCI, is a short, professional email sent to the admissions office that communicates three things: you are still interested in attending, you would accept an offer of admission if extended, and you have meaningful updates to share since you submitted your application.

The letter should be brief. A page or less. Thank the admissions committee for continuing to consider your application. State clearly that the school remains your first choice and you will enroll if admitted. Then provide one or two specific updates. A new award you won. A leadership role you took on. A significant project you completed. Improved grades from your most recent semester. The updates should be concrete and recent, not a rehashing of what was already in your application.

Close by reaffirming your interest and thanking them again for their time. Attach the letter as a PDF and paste it into the body of the email as well. Address it to the admissions officer responsible for your region if you know their name. If not, address it to the Director of Admissions.

What Not to Do

Do not send multiple letters. One well-written LOCI is sufficient. Additional emails will annoy the admissions office and make you look unable to follow instructions. Do not send gifts, baked goods, or anything other than a letter. Do not have your parents call the admissions office on your behalf. Do not camp out in the admissions parking lot. These are real examples of things applicants have done. They did not work.

Do not treat the LOCI as an opportunity to explain why the admissions committee made a mistake. They did not make a mistake. They made a decision based on institutional needs and a competitive applicant pool. Your letter should be positive and forward-looking, not defensive or resentful.

Step Four: Update Your Grades

If you are currently enrolled in classes, have your high school send updated transcripts at the end of the semester. Strong senior-year grades demonstrate that you are continuing to work hard and are prepared for college-level academics. A rising grade trend can be a meaningful factor in waitlist decisions.

Step Five: Move On Emotionally

Once you have accepted your waitlist spot, sent your LOCI, and deposited at another school, let go. Emotionally commit to the school where you enrolled. Decorate your dorm room in your mind. Join the accepted students social media group. Start imagining your life there. If the waitlist school calls in June, it will be a happy surprise. If they do not, you are already at a school you chose and are excited about.

The Bottom Line

Being waitlisted is disappointing, but it is not a rejection. Accept your spot on the waitlist. Pay a deposit at another school you like. Write one strong Letter of Continued Interest with specific updates. Submit your final grades. Then move forward emotionally with the school that said yes. A waitlist decision in June will either be a bonus or irrelevant. Either way, you are going to college in the fall, and that is the goal.
